title: Point Load Test
aim: to determine the indirect tensile strength of rock using Brazillian test.
theory: Brazilian test is used to determine the tensile strength of rock indirectly.
Direct tensile testing of rocks is difficult to perform, so this method is preferred.
A cylindrical disc-shaped specimen is used in the test.
The specimen is placed horizontally between two loading platens.
Load is applied diametrically (along the diameter of the disc).
This creates tensile stress perpendicular to the applied load direction.
Although the load is compressive, it induces tensile failure inside the specimen.
The rock fails by splitting along the vertical diameter.
The tensile strength depends on applied load at failure, diameter of specimen, and thickness of specimen.
The tensile strength is calculated using the formula: σt = (2P) / (πDt).
It is an indirect method for evaluating tensile strength of rocks.
The test is simple, quick, and widely used in rock mechanics.
procedure: A cylindrical rock specimen (disc) is prepared with uniform diameter and thickness.
The specimen surfaces are made smooth and parallel to ensure proper loading.
The diameter (D) and thickness (t) of the specimen are measured accurately.
The specimen is placed horizontally between the loading platens of the testing machine.
It is aligned such that the load is applied along the diameter of the disc.
Thin packing strips (cardboard or plywood) may be placed between platens and specimen to ensure uniform load distribution.
Load is applied gradually and continuously without shock.
The load is increased until the specimen fails (splits along the vertical diameter).
The failure load (P) is recorded at the moment of fracture.
The test is repeated for multiple specimens for accuracy.
The tensile strength is calculated using the standard formula.
The average value of tensile strength is reported as the result.
result: Tensile Strength of the Sample is 0.256 KN/cm^2
